coredata.dc:title: Dual-excitation carbon dots-based lateral flow visual sensing device for ratiometric determination and discrimination of tetracyclines in food
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Guoqiang
Li, Tingting
Zheng, Qi
Tang, Siyuan
Hu, Houwen
Wang, Xu
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: Excessive releasing of tetracyclines in the nature has constituted a major threat to ecosystem. Therefore, it is crucial to develop an accurate and convenient on-site analysis device for tetracyclines. Herein, we construct a neoteric dual-excitation carbon dots (DE-CDs) through microwave radiation and establish an excitation-based ratiometric approach for quantitative tetracyclines determination. The ratio of different excitation intensities under the same emission wavelength changes linearly in response to tetracyclines concentration. As a proof of feasibility, we choose doxycycline (DOX) as the analysis model. It shows a low detection limit (LOD) of 61 nM in a wide concentration range of 0.061–80 µM, suggesting that this sensor is conducive to application potential in tetracyclines determination of food products. Moreover, considering the differentiated overlap between tetracyclines absorbance and DE-CDs excitations, the discrimination of tetracyclines is realized in the form of mixtures. Significantly, combining with the DE-CDs, RGB (Red, Green and Blue) analysis and 3D printing technologies, we construct a miniaturized visual sensing device with low LOD and achieve the discrimination of TCs based on RGB variations, showing a great opportunity for industrial production of pollutant monitoring sensors in resource-constrained areas.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-excitation carbon dots
Tetracyclines
Excitation-based ratiometric sensing
On-site quantitation and discrimination
3D printing technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and utilization of the smartphone-integrated visual on-site sensing device
Establishment and detection principle of visual sensing device
Detection performance and selectivity analysis of DE-CDs
Discrimination of TCs by excitation-based ratiometric method
Visual detection and discrimination of TCs using the designed sensing device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The establishment route of visual sensing device. (b) The process of immobilizing DE-CDs into the sensing strip. (c) Schematic diagram of the proposed excitation-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing device for the visualization of TCs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of DE-CDs. (b) Emission spectra of DE-CDs with the excitation wavelength increasing from 350 to 550 nm. (c) Effects of ultraviolet radiation, saline solution concentration and storage time on the DE-CDs fluorescence.

(a) Excitation-emission matrix graph map of DE-CDs in the presence of DOX. (b) Excitation spectra of DE-CDs upon the addition of DOX. (c) Plot of I
465
/I
385 versus DOX from 0 to 200 µM and linear relationship in the range of 0–80 µM. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of DE-CDs, DOX, DE-CDs/DOX and DE-CDs + DOX (the sum curve). (e) UV–vis absorption of DOX, excitation and emission spectra of DE-CDs. (f) Fluorescence lifetimes of DE-CDs in the existence and inexistence of DOX. (g) The mechanism diagram of DE-CDs response to TCs.

(a) Canonical score plots for the discrimination of TCs in the total concentration of 30 µM using LDA analysis. (b) HCA plot about the discrimination of TCs at 30 µM. (c) Canonical score plots for the discrimination of two TCs mixture (30 µM). (d) The canonical score plots for the discrimination of two TCs mixtures (30 µM).

(a) The visual sensing and discrimination route of the designed device. (b) Plot of Sum
2
/Sum
1 with DOX concentration changing in range of 0–100 µM and fitting curve from 0 to 47 µM. (c) The digital images of DE-CDs solution upon the addition of DOX with two excitation lamps. (d) Applications of the sensing strip for TCs detection. (e) LDA and HCA analysis for the discrimination of TCs.
Schematic illustration of the construction process of excitation-based ratiometric sensor and the application for the determination and discrimination of TCs based on RGB analysis.

head.ce:title: Dual-excitation carbon dots-based lateral flow visual sensing device for ratiometric determination and discrimination of tetracyclines in food

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Conceptualization, Methodology, Computation, Writing – original draft
Writing – review & editing, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive releasing of tetracyclines in the nature has constituted a major threat to ecosystem. Therefore, it is crucial to develop an accurate and convenient on-site analysis device for tetracyclines. Herein, we construct a neoteric dual-excitation carbon dots (DE-CDs) through microwave radiation and establish an excitation-based ratiometric approach for quantitative tetracyclines determination. The ratio of different excitation intensities under the same emission wavelength changes linearly in response to tetracyclines concentration. As a proof of feasibility, we choose doxycycline (DOX) as the analysis model. It shows a low detection limit (LOD) of 61 nM in a wide concentration range of 0.061–80 µM, suggesting that this sensor is conducive to application potential in tetracyclines determination of food products. Moreover, considering the differentiated overlap between tetracyclines absorbance and DE-CDs excitations, the discrimination of tetracyclines is realized in the form of mixtures. Significantly, combining with the DE-CDs, RGB (Red, Green and Blue) analysis and 3D printing technologies, we construct a miniaturized visual sensing device with low LOD and achieve the discrimination of TCs based on RGB variations, showing a great opportunity for industrial production of pollutant monitoring sensors in resource-constrained areas.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As broad-spectrum antibiotics, tetracycline antibiotics (TCs) are industrially produced by actinomycetes for human therapy, animal disease control and agricultural feed additives. They mainly include doxycycline (DOX), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline (TC), etc, all of which have similar structures.[1] Due to the widespread use of TCs as drugs and growth promoters, massive residues have constituted a major threat in the environmental system. In such a case, TCs can reach the human body through surface waters and even drinking water, affecting the internal organs of the human body and restricting the bones and teeth developments [2,3]. Moreover, diverse tetracyclines existing in the environment in the form of mixtures have disastrous consequences for the eco-environment restoration because of the difficulties from discriminating the species of TCs [4]. Therefore, exploring the high-performance strategy for monitoring and discrimination of TCs is of great moment to the protection of the biosecurity and ecology environment.

Given the influences of TCs toward health and ecotope, numerous assays have been developed for monitoring TCs, including electrochemistry analysis [5], enzyme-linked immunoassay [6], capillary electrophoresis (CEP) [7], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [8] and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9]. However, they suffer from some inevitable problems during the detection, such as poor repeatability, low operation cost, simple pre-treatment, prolonged detection time, elaborate sample preparation procedures and skilled personnel technology and so on. In contrast, fluorimetry stands out from the crowd due to its excellent detection performance [10,11]. Among most of fluorescence materials, carbon dots (CDs), as a newly-developing star of carbon nanomaterials, have drawn more attention from researchers because of their chemical inertness, superior fluorescence characteristic, good solubility and hypotoxicity, etc [12,13]. For sensitive detection of TCs, many researchers have constructed various analytical strategies based on single or multiple emission signals through the chemical conjugation, surface modification or pairing between absorber and fluorophore [14–16]. Among these analytical strategies, methods based on multiple signals ratiometric variations are more appropriate for actual applications owing to the built-in self-calibration against the interferences of instrumental and environmental factors. On the other hand, overwhelming majority of fluorescence sensors stick to analysis based on emission signals while analytical methods depending on fluorescence excitation information have rarely been reported, which is inconducive to the diversity and innovativeness of sensors. Therefore, it is challengeable and profound to develop a ratiometric CDs sensor based on excitation signals for the detection of TCs.

In recent years, varieties of portable sensing device are developed, which receive praises because of convenience and low-cost [17,18]. These on-site sensing platforms have a good potential in detection of TCs whereas lacking of anti-interference and difficulties for real-time quantitative determination greatly limit their practicability. It still leaves room for improvement. Inspired by the Corona Virus Disease 2019 antigen detection kit, its pattern of detection and control lines effectively overcome the emergence of false positive results. Meanwhile, combining with the image processing capability of smartphone, it will show a great opportunity for popularization and application of pollutant monitoring sensors. In addition, to the best of our knowledge, three-dimensional (3D) printed technology enables to maximize the implement of the sensing device configuration and minimize the cost for constructing sensing devices. Consequently, it is far-reaching and meaningful to develop a visual real-time quantitative device by combining with the suitable fluorescence sensors, smartphone and 3D printed technology in the environmental monitoring.

Herein, the dual-excitation carbon dots (DE-CDs) are synthesized by microwave radiation of melamine and o-phenylenediamine (OPD). The as-prepared DE-CDs exhibit an intensive yellow fluorescence with an excellent stability. Notably, it shows a neoteric concentration-dependent dual-excitation behavior that regulating the DE-CDs concentration enables the excitation peak to split and shift toward 385 nm and 465 nm, respectively. Based on this unique behavior, an excitation-based ratiometric sensor is achieved ( Scheme 1). The long-wavelength excitation peak is determined as reference signal while the short-wavelength excitation peak as a response signal sensitively responses to the concentration of TCs due to the uneven overlap of DE-CDs excitation and TCs absorbance. The proposed analytical method exhibits low limits of detection (LODs) of 61 nM, 72 nM, 88 nM and 115 nM for DOX, CTC, OTC and TC, respectively. Moreover, the discrimination of TCs is performed by the canonical score plots and displays the satisfying results. Meanwhile, inspired by the smartphone’s image processing and 3D printed technologies, a smartphone-integrated visual on-site sensing device with self-calibration function is successfully established. On the basis of the chromogenic reaction from control line (valid data) and the ratiometric RGB (Red, Green and Blue) variation of two lines (Test-1 and Test-2), the excellent detection performances are obtained and verified by low LODs and satisfying recoveries of TCs. Furthermore, this device also realizes the precise distinguishment of TCs species and even the mixtures of TCs. Thus, our work implements a jump from fluorescence materials to systematic sensing device, maximizing the possibility of industrial production of pollutant monitoring sensors in resource-constrained areas.
Details about the materials, instruments, characterizations and procedure were shown in the Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and utilization of the smartphone-integrated visual on-site sensing device
ce:section.ce:para: The smartphone-integrated visual on-site sensing device developed in this work consists of sample loading platform using the Computer Aided Design (CAD), smartphone and the sensing strip (Supporting Information). The sensing strip has two test-lines for the determination and discrimination of TCs with self-calibration function. It is worth mentioning that the design of smartphone integration and self-calibration function improves the feasibility and efficiency of this device. Finally, these components are assembled to complete the fabrication of the smartphone-integrated visual on-site sensing device which is applied in food and water samples analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment and detection principle of visual sensing device
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the high efficiency of Corona Virus Disease 2019 antigen detection kit, a portable and low-cost sensing device is significant for the requirement of pollutant monitoring in the environment. Thus, we develop a self-calibration on-site sensing device for TCs residuals in food products. To begin with, the appearance and structure of the sensing device are designed in the CAD. These files formats are converted into the standard triangle language (.stl) format, which can be identified by 3D printer [19,20] Whereafter, the three-dimensional model is printed layer by layer. The whole process is displayed in Fig. 1(a). As shown in Fig. 1(b), the DE-CDs are immobilized in nitrocellulose membrane to formulate Test-1 and Test-2 lines for the self-calibration on-site sensing, and then this sensing strip is embedded in the 3D printed device. Thus, the miniaturized visual sensing device is successfully designed. For the determination of TCs, as shown in Fig. 1(c), the pretreated solutions are dropped on the sample pad. Due to the capillarity, the analyte starts to flow transversely toward the Test-1 and Test-2 of detection area, and eventually flows to the control line. The data of Test-1 and Test-2 is valid for statistics on condition that the chromogenic reaction occurs in the control line. In the existence of TCs, the fluorescence of Test-1 and Test-2 lines differentially responses to the TCs concentration under the different excitation lamps irradiation. Based on these results, smartphone converts fluorescence signals to RGB ratio values, and further establishes the internal relationship of TCs concentration and RGB ratio value to realize the quantitatively analysis.

The morphology, structure, chemical constituents of DE-CDs are characterized via a series of characterizations (Fig. S1-S3). These relevant results and discussions are presented in the Supporting Information. To furtherly study the fluorescence characteristic of DE-CDs, the UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra are monitored. Fig. 2(a) displays an intensive absorption at 270–300 nm relating to the π-π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 domains while the dwarf absorption band of 350–470 nm is assigned to the n-π* transitions of nitrogen/oxygen-related states [21]. Under the irradiation of 365 nm, the yellowish-brown pellucid solution emits a bright yellow fluorescence. Notably, the fluorescence spectra show the dual-excitation band of 380–490 nm but merely an emission peak at about 570 nm. With the increase of excitation wavelength from 350 to 550 nm, the emission spectra clearly show the fluorescence intensity changes instead of wavelength, indicating that DE-CDs possess an excitation-independent luminescence behavior (Fig. 2(b)). According to the previous reports, it can be concluded that this neoteric dual-excitation characteristic is closely concerned with the structural property of DE-CDs [22,23]. In general, nitrogen/oxygen doping can lead to the generation of new energy levels and electric potential changing in DE-CDs. As the DE-CDs concentration increases, the energy level will split and broaden, resulting in the occurrence of dual-excitation phenomenon. Moreover, for verifying the bimodal excitation origin of DE-CDs, a controlled experiment is implemented which can be found in the Supporting Information (Fig. S4 and Fig. S5). These nitrogen/oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of DE-CDs interact with each other and further result in the concentration-induced bimodal excitation behavior with the concentration of DE-CDs changing 24]. Fig. S6 shows the controllability of distance between the two excitation peaks. Additionally, the fluorescence stability under various situations is also studied. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the optimum emission intensity is almost insusceptible to the external factors, even when the DE-CDs are exposed to ultraviolet radiation for 18 h or the concentration of saline solution up to 2.4 mol‧L−1. And after the 24 days of storage, the DE-CDs have a little intensity variation comparing with the original fluorescence intensity. Hence, the neoteric DE-CDs provide a reliable foundation for the construction of an excitation-based ratiometric sensor with excellent photo-stabilities in various environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection performance and selectivity analysis of DE-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Considering that the complexity of liquids in authentic environment, we investigate the selectivity of DE-CDs towards TCs before detection analysis. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence excitation intensities (I
465 and I
385 , I
465 and I
385 denote the intensities of excitation signals at 465 nm and 385 nm) of DE-CDs solution introduced by other substances (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, I-, Br-, NO3 -, CO3 2-, SO4 2-, enrofloxacin (ENR), ciprofloxacin (CIP), pefloxacin (PEF), fleroxacin (FLE) and marbofloxacin (MAR); 500 µM) instead of TCs (DOX, CTC, OTC and TC; 100 µM) are monitored under the emission of 570 nm. Remarkably, the results reflect that the dual-excitation signals are hardly subject to other interferents even when their concentrations are up to 500 µM. However, DOX, CTC, OTC and TC cause significant effect on the I
385 of DE-CDs whereas the I
465 has negligible changes. The experiment result shows that DE-CDs have a high selectivity to TCs and response differentially to the different species of TCs.

Subsequently, DOX is selected as the analysis representative to explain the detection process and sensing principle of TCs. As depicted in Fig. 3(a), it illustrates that the fluorescence intensities of excitation peaks decrease differentially upon the addition of DOX. In such a case, we determine the short-wavelength excitation (385 nm) as response signal and the long-wavelength excitation (465 nm) as reference signal for establishing a type of excitation-based ratiometric method. It can be seen in Fig. 3(b) that the response signal at 385 nm continues to decline with the increasement of DOX content while the signal at 465 has a little fluctuation. The plot of I
465
/I
385 versus DOX in the concentration of 0–200 µM and fitting curve from 0 to 80 µM are conducted, and the LOD of DOX is calculated to be 61 nM through the equation: [23].
(1)
LOD = 3 σ / k
where σ and k are the signal standard deviation and slope respectively (Fig. 3(c)). Similarly, CTC, OTC and TC are also detected with concentrations of respective targets in the certain range, 0.072–105 µM for CTC, 0.088–110 µM for OTC and 0.115–80 µM for TC. The LODs of CTC, OTC and TC are calculated to be 72 nM, 88 nM and 115 nM, respectively (Fig. S8). By comparison with other technologies in Table S1, it reveals that our proposed excitation-based ratiometric method is comparable in both LODs and detection ranges, and has a great potential in application of monitoring TCs species.

Furthermore, in view of the above phenomena about fluorescence quenching, some experiments are performed for the sake of investigating the interaction between DE-CDs and DOX. Combining with the FT-IR and XPS data, the DE-CDs surface possesses a great deal of hydrophilic groups. Meanwhile, the abundant hydroxyl and amino groups of DOX are conducive to generate the strong hydrogen bonds with DE-CDs. Thus, to evaluate the interaction strength between DE-CDs and TCs, the binding constants (K) of TCs (7.06 ×104 M−1 for DOX, 1.02 ×105 M−1 for CTC, 6.75 ×104 M−1 for OTC and 2.92 ×104 M−1 for TC) are calculated (Fig. S9), and their values are much higher than other ions, according to the Benesi-Hildebrand Eq. (1): [25].
(2)
1 / ( I +
I
0
) = 1 / [ K
I
max
−
I
0
C ] + 1 / (
I
max
−
I
0
)
where C represents the targets concentration;
I
0
denotes the fluorescence intensity of the mixtures; I and
I
max
are the fluorescence intensity after adding TCs of the instant and saturated concentrations, respectively. These results illustrate that the high selectivity and sensitivity of sensors for detecting TCs originate from some binding interactions. Additionally, Fig. 3(d) indicates that there is no complex formed between DE-CDs and DOX by comparing the absorption spectrum (curve iii) of DE-CDs after adding DOX and the physical addition spectrum of curve i + curve ii (curve iv). It is well known that the occurrence of inner filter effect (IFE) merely requires the spectral overlap without the formation of new compound. Fig. 3(e) shows that the absorption spectrum of DOX overlaps locally with the excitation spectrum of DE-CDs, and the short-wavelength excitation peak declines accompanied with the addition of DOX whereas the long-wavelength excitation peak has a little fluctuation. Based on the uneven overlap of DE-CDs excitation and DOX absorption spectrum, IFE is inferred to be the domination of differentiated fluorescence quenching [26,27]. Interestingly, with the localization of fluorescence quenching, it is identified to be the localized inner filter effect (L-IFE). To further verify this assumption, the fluorescence lifetime measurements are performed. It is obviously that the fluorescence lifetimes of DE-CDs in the existence and inexistence of DOX vary in a negligible range regardless of being excited at 385 nm or 465 nm (Fig. 3(f)). Thus, DOX can not only form the strong hydrogen-bond with DE-CDs but also absorb the energy competitively due to the L-IFE leading to the fluorescence quenching, thereby achieving the detection of DOX. The measurement results of other TCs are nearly similar which are shown in Fig. S10, and the mechanism diagram of DE-CDs response to TCs is displayed in Fig. 3(g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of TCs by excitation-based ratiometric method
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the different excitation signals response to TCs, the excitation-based analysis is used for discriminating the various TCs species at the total concentration of 30 µM. According to the previous reports, the linear discriminant analysis (LDA) enables data to be separated maximally by increasing the variance ratio from two or more types of events [28]. After transforming the multiset data into the typical factors by LDA, Fig. 4(a) presents four groups that cluster the typical patterns of the analytical results corresponding to the various analytes without overlap. Meanwhile, through the hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) classification method, TCs are well clustered into the respective groups in Fig. 4(b). Furthermore, in order to get closer to the realistic situation, the two TCs mixtures (30 µM) are analyzed. Similarly, as we expect, 25 cases are accurately classified into their respectively groups with the 100% variance in Fig. 4(c-d). The LDA and HCA data verify the high accuracy and feasibility of our proposed method, even when in more complicated cases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection and discrimination of TCs using the designed sensing device
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the overuse of TCs and the expensiveness of analytical tool, the low-cost, portable and high-efficiency sensing device for detection and discrimination of TCs is a growing demand. Therefore, we utilize the 3D printing technology to construct a sensing device with the low-cost polylactic acid material. Meanwhile, on the detection performance of ensuring, the 385 nm and 465 nm lamps are selected as excitation sources for further minimizing the cost of the portable device. And the built-in two-line (Test-1 and Test-2) design can visually detect and discriminate TCs with high accuracy.

For the detection of TCs, the whole sensing flow is shown in the Fig. 5(a). First, we select the DOX as the analysis model, and prepare DOX solution at different concentrations (0–100 µM) for testing experiments. Then, drop the solution onto the inlet of the sensing strip and keep quiescence for 10 min. During the process of lateral flow, it is obvious that the analyte flow through the Test-1 and then transfers to the Test-2 and control line. After the occurrence of chromogenic reaction in control line (from colorless to bluish violet color), the data will be valid for statistics. Under the irradiation of two excitation sources, the fluorescence of line generates the differentiated variations. Furtherly, the RGB analysis software of smartphone converts the brightness variations into the RGB values (Sum= Red + Blue + Green; Sum
1 and Sum
2 denote the RGB values under the excitation of 385 nm and 465 nm, respectively). After that, these parameters are used to establish a good linear relationship with DOX concentration (Fig. 5(b)). The digital images of DE-CDs solution upon the addition of DOX with two excitation lamps is presented in Fig. 5(c). In addition, real samples spiked by DOX at different concentrations are analyzed, and the satisfying recoveries are obtained in Table S2. Also, encouraged by the excellent detection performance of DOX (LOD=99.8 nM), other TCs are also monitored in the similar steps. As presented in Fig. S11, the LODs of CTC, OTC and TC are calculated to be 158 nM, 124 nM and 176 nM in respective ranges.

For the discrimination of TCs, this device is used to measure four TCs (50 µM) and the mixtures containing DOX and CTC with different molar ratio in a total concentration of 50 µM. Through the LDA and HCA analysis, all samples are well discriminated to the corresponding groups with the 100% variance (Fig. 5 (d)). Therefore, the designed sensing strip can response to the specific response signals for TCs, and these experiments demonstrate the excellent feasibility of this sensing strip to detect and discriminate the TCs species.

In this study, the DE-CDs are synthesized successfully via microwave radiation using OPD and melamine as raw materials, which exhibits not only an excellent fluorescence stability but also the neoteric concentration-dependency dual-excitation behavior. By means of regulating the DE-CDs concentration, the two excitation peaks are separated to the suitable sites (385 nm and 465 nm), being conducive to the uneven overlap with the TCs absorbance. Based on these phenomena, the DE-CDs are designed as an excitation-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor to detect TCs due to the L-IFE. And the satisfying results with LODs of 61 nM for DOX, 72 nM for CTC, 88 nM for OTC and 115 nM for TC are further obtained in the same analysis steps. Simultaneously, the specific response of DE-CDs to four TCs are utilized to discriminate them and even their mixtures with different molar ratio. The LDA and HCA results verify the feasibility and high accuracy of our approach well. Furtherly, considering the practical use, a visual self-calibration sensing device is developed by 3D printer and combines with smartphone to achieve the rapid detection and discrimination of four TCs via RGB analysis. Consequently, this work provides a possibility of developing a novel excitation-based ratiometric sensing device for visual determination and discrimination of four TCs, as well as playing an important role in daily food safety evaluation and pollution monitoring.

Guoqiang Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Computation, Writing – original draft preparation. Tingting Li: Conceptualization, Investigation. Qi Zheng: Data curation, Investigation. Siyuan Tang: Data curation, Software. Houwen Hu: Investigation. Xu Wang: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Da Chen: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134523.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A rotating paper-based microfluidic sensor array combining Michael acceptors and carbon quantum dots for discrimination of biothiols
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhu, X.C. Mei, Z.C. Peng, J. Liu, J. Yang, Y.C. Li, A rotating paper-based microfluidic sensor array combining Michael acceptors and carbon quantum dots for discrimination of biothiols, Chem. Eng. J. 454 (2023) 140065.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Guoqiang Guo is a master student under the guidance of Professor Da Chen at Ningbo University in China. His current research interests focus on the preparing of graphene quantum dots and their applications.
Tingting Li is a master student under the guidance of Professor Da Chen at Ningbo University in China. Her research interests include graphene quantum dots detection method.
Qi Zheng is a master student under the guidance of Professor Xu Wang at Ningbo University in China. His current research interests focus on the semiconductor device design.
Siyuan Tang is a master student under the guidance of Professor Da Chen at Ningbo University in China. His current research interests focus on the study of luminescence mechanism.

Houwen Hu is a master student under the guidance of Professor Da Chen at Ningbo University in China. His research interests include the development of optoelectronic devices based on graphene quantum dots.

Xu Wang is a professor at Ningbo University in China. He received his Doctor of Engineering degree from Japan National Saga University in 201. Professor Xu Wang's research interests mainly focus on the epitaxial growth of wide band gap semiconductor and the development of optoelectronic devices.

Da Chen is a professor at Ningbo University in China. He received his Doctor of Engineering degree from Lanzhou University in 2015. Professor Chen Da's research interests mainly focus on the preparation of novel functional materials and device research.
